goal: Make shared success, empty, pending, and failure states feel like product recovery moments with visible actions, clear labels, and accessible route-state checks.

success_criteria.description: shared/ui/state-view.tsx accepts typed recovery actions or links and renders them as visible controls with accessible names; empty, pending, and failure states no longer rely on plain text instructions for actions the user cannot take.

shared/ui/app-shell.tsx renders scenario or recovery links as secondary demo checks with visible labels, never as empty anchors, and keeps them outside the primary action order on desktop and 375px mobile.

GET /app?scenario=empty, /app?scenario=pending, /app?scenario=failure, /app/profile?scenario=empty, /app/profile?scenario=pending, and /app/profile?scenario=failure expose meaningful state headings and at least one visible recovery control whose label matches the recovery copy.

tests/ui/state-view.test.tsx and tests/pages/product-state-recovery.test.tsx assert accessible names for state controls, no empty state links, no invisible action instructions, and no horizontal overflow assumptions for long recovery labels.

Existing app-shell, app-page, app-profile, and product-copy tests are updated only for intentional state/recovery behavior; npm test and npm run build pass with no backend, service, API, or route-handler edits.

out_of_scope: Changing API envelopes or route handlers
Changing source/provenance component architecture from Sprint 72
Rewriting route-specific product layout reserved for Sprints 74-79
Changing sample data fixtures or service factories
